primary_diagnosis.name: Acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage with hypovolaemic (haemorrhagic) shock, presumed peptic-ulcer or alcohol-related upper GI source

comorbidities.name: Alcohol use disorder / hazardous alcohol use

pathophysiology_summary: Coffee-ground haematemesis together with melaena localises an actively bleeding source to the upper gastrointestinal tract (proximal to the ligament of Treitz). The most likely lesions given heavy alcohol use and NSAID exposure are a bleeding peptic (gastric or duodenal) ulcer or NSAID-induced gastric erosion; oesophago-gastric varices are an important alternative in a heavy drinker with possible portal hypertension. Ongoing blood loss has produced intravascular volume depletion: postural near-syncope, weakness, pallor and diaphoresis are the clinical signature of early hypovolaemic (haemorrhagic) shock, with compensatory tachycardia and a narrowing/falling blood pressure. Untreated, continued haemorrhage leads to decompensated shock, end-organ hypoperfusion and death. Definitive control requires resuscitation (IV crystalloid and blood products), acid suppression and urgent upper GI endoscopy for diagnosis and haemostasis; in a suspected variceal bleed, vasoactive therapy and antibiotics are added. None of these are deliverable by a telehealth AI Doctor — the correct action is immediate emergency-ambulance escalation with interim positioning and airway-protection advice.

differential.evidence_basis: Coffee-ground vomiting plus black tarry stools plus near-syncope on standing is a classic major upper-GI bleed with haemodynamic compromise.
Preceding burning epigastric pain plus NSAID use points to a peptic-ulcer source as the most probable lesion.
Heavy long-standing alcohol use raises the possibility of portal hypertension and varices, which change hospital management (vasoactive drugs, antibiotics) though not the telehealth action.
Considered and argued against: coffee-ground haematemesis with melaena localises the source to the upper GI tract, not the lower bowel.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At first contact the AI Doctor should already recognise a probable major upper-GI bleed with shock and be moving to escalate — precise lesion identification is a hospital/endoscopy task, not a prerequisite for the emergency response.

differential.diagnosis: Acute upper gastrointestinal bleed with hypovolaemic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Haematemesis plus melaena plus postural collapse plus pallor/diaphoresis confirms active haemorrhage with early shock.

differential.diagnosis: Black stools from oral iron or bismuth (pseudomelaena)
differential.evidence_basis: Excluded by the accompanying true haematemesis and haemodynamic compromise — dietary discolouration does not cause tachycardia, pallor and near-syncope.
Excluded by frank blood in the vomit and tarry stools with shock and no fever — this is haemorrhage, not infective gastroenteritis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag cluster is unambiguous; the failure mode to guard against is under-action (managing at home, advising oral fluids/self-transport), not misdiagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: Acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage with hypovolaemic shock (presumed peptic-ulcer or alcohol-related source)
differential.evidence_basis: Time-critical active upper-GI bleed with early shock; definitive source and haemostasis require urgent hospital endoscopy after resuscitation.

red_flags.description: Coffee-ground / blood haematemesis — active upper gastrointestinal bleeding
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms an active upper-GI bleeding source; volume and rate of loss determine how fast the patient can decompensate.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise haemorrhage leads to under-triage and continued unmonitored blood loss.

red_flags.description: Melaena — black tarry stools indicating digested upper-GI blood
red_flags.clinical_significance: Corroborates a significant upper-GI bleed with enough volume and transit time to produce melaena.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing melaena as blood risks dismissing the presentation as benign GI upset.

red_flags.description: Postural near-syncope, weakness, pallor and diaphoresis — early hypovolaemic (haemorrhagic) shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals that blood loss has exceeded compensatory reserve; this is the feature that makes the case an immediate emergency, not a same-day review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage here risks fatal exsanguination or hypoperfusion-related cardiac arrest before help arrives.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured that black stools are just from iron tablets or diet, and missing that accompanying haematemesis plus shock confirm true haemorrhage.
Framing coffee-ground vomiting and tummy pain as gastritis or gastroenteritis suitable for a same-day GP review, rather than a shocked patient needing an ambulance now.

Advising the patient to drink fluids to replace losses or take an antacid — this delays escalation, risks aspiration in a vomiting patient, and jeopardises the airway and impending endoscopy (patient must stay nil-by-mouth).
Telling a haemodynamically unstable, near-fainting patient to drive or be driven to hospital — collapse or arrest en route is a real risk; this is a 000 ambulance case.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High-risk (markedly elevated) — melaena, syncope, tachycardia and haemodynamic instability all score; a score this high mandates admission and urgent intervention
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Identifies a patient who cannot be managed in the community and needs emergency resuscitation and endoscopy; the AI Doctor cannot compute the full score without bloods (urea, haemoglobin) but the clinical features alone place the patient in the highest-urgency band.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact blood pressure and heart rate (source records BP 90/60, HR 118 — clinician-measured, not available via chat without a home device)
Capillary refill time / peripheral perfusion (source: 4 seconds — clinician-observed)
Abdominal examination findings — epigastric tenderness, absence of rigidity/rebound, hyperactive bowel sounds
Digital rectal examination confirming melaena on the glove
Haemoglobin level and the anticipated significant drop (requires venepuncture and lab)
Upper GI endoscopy findings — actively bleeding mucosal lesion or visible vessel
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 emergency-ambulance dispatch (Priority 1). Interim advice a carer can carry out: keep the patient lying flat with legs elevated, strictly nil-by-mouth, do not let him stand or walk, and place him in the recovery position if he vomits again or loses consciousness. The AI Doctor cannot cannulate, give IV fluids or blood, prescribe acid suppression, or perform endoscopy — these are hospital actions the ambulance and ED provide.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the syndrome (haematemesis + melaena + shock is unambiguous). The real risk is UNDER-action — under-triaging to a GP visit, advising oral fluids/antacids, or recommending self-transport instead of an emergency ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE CG141 — Acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding in over 16s: management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Risk-assess with Glasgow-Blatchford at first presentation; resuscitate unstable patients and arrange urgent (within 24h, immediately if unstable) upper GI endoscopy; the community/telehealth role is recognition and emergency escalation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: British Society of Gastroenterology acute upper GI bleeding care bundle
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Haemodynamically unstable upper-GI bleeding is a medical emergency requiring immediate resuscitation and time-critical endoscopy; consider variceal source in patients with chronic liver disease/alcohol use.
